items.html:
Welcome to Standard 8

Welcome to Fluids and Nutrition, the knowledge element of Standard 8 of the Care Certificate (2025 framework, England). Helping people to eat and drink well is one of the most basic human needs, and one of the most important things you will support people with on almost every shift. Done well, it protects health, aids recovery and helps people feel valued and comfortable.

  • Hydration: helping people drink enough to stay well.
  • Nutrition: supporting people to eat a balanced diet they enjoy.
  • Dignity: making mealtimes safe, sociable and person-centred.

What this course covers

Over the next pages you will learn why food and fluids matter, how to meet people's hydration and nutrition needs, and how to support them safely and kindly at mealtimes — including those who find swallowing difficult. You will also see how to spot the early signs of dehydration and malnutrition, and what to do when you are worried about someone. This standard is about supporting people to eat and drink well; a separate Food Hygiene course covers safe food handling in full.

Take your time as you work through, and think about the people you support and how each idea applies to them. This e-learning covers the knowledge element of Standard 8 only. To achieve the Care Certificate, your employer must also observe your competence in real work and sign it off — My Care Academy cannot award the Care Certificate on its own.

More than fuel

Good food and drink keep the body working. They give people energy, help wounds heal, protect against infection and keep muscles and bones strong enough to stay active and independent. When someone is eating and drinking poorly, they tire quickly, recover slowly and are far more likely to become unwell.

  • Health: supports recovery, immunity and energy.
  • Wellbeing: a good meal lifts mood and comfort.
  • Connection: mealtimes bring people together.

Food, culture and identity

Food is never only about nutrients. What we eat is tied to who we are — our religion, culture, family and memories. A favourite meal can bring comfort on a hard day, and sharing food is one of the main ways people stay connected to others.

Because of this, offering people real choice about what, when and how they eat is part of treating them with dignity. A meal someone enjoys and has chosen does far more good than one they simply tolerate. Losing the ability to eat and drink the things you love, or to eat in the way you always have, can feel like a real loss, so the sensitivity you bring to mealtimes matters just as much as the food itself.

items.question: Why is it important to see food and drink as more than just nutrition?

answers.text: Because meals are also linked to comfort, culture, identity and social connection, all of which support wellbeing.
answers.feedback: Yes. Enjoyment, choice and social contact matter as much as the nutrients themselves.

answers.text: Because the calories in food are the only thing that affects health.
answers.feedback: Nutrition matters, but food also shapes mood, dignity and social life.

answers.text: Because everyone should eat exactly the same balanced diet.
answers.feedback: People's tastes, cultures and needs differ — one diet does not suit all.

answers.text: Because mealtimes are mainly a chance to get tasks finished quickly.
answers.feedback: Rushing mealtimes removes the comfort and connection that make them valuable.

items.html:
Your role at mealtimes

As a care worker, you are often the person who notices first when someone is not eating or drinking as they should. Your role is to offer, encourage and support people to eat and drink well according to their care plan, and to watch closely for any changes.

  • Offer: present food and drink people like, in the way they prefer.
  • Encourage: gently prompt and support without forcing.
  • Observe: notice how much someone eats, drinks and enjoys.

Knowing your limits

Supporting people well also means knowing where your role ends. You should follow the person's care plan, but you should not change someone's diet, decide they need supplements, or give specialist dietary advice yourself. Those decisions belong to nurses, dietitians and speech and language therapists.

What you must always do is follow the care plan and report concerns promptly. If you are worried that someone is losing weight, refusing food or drink, or struggling to swallow, tell a senior colleague or nurse so the right professional can assess them. You never need all the answers yourself; noticing a change and passing it on quickly is exactly what is expected of you, and it can make a real difference to the person's health.

items.question: If you are worried that someone is not eating or drinking enough, you should keep it to yourself until you are completely certain there is a problem.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Report concerns early — noticing and passing on changes is a key part of your role.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Waiting until you are certain can delay help. Report changes early so someone can assess the person.

items.html:
Why water matters

The body is mostly water, and it needs a steady supply to work properly. Fluids help the blood carry nutrients, keep the kidneys flushing out waste, control body temperature and keep the mind clear. Even mild dehydration can leave someone tired, muddled and unwell.

  • Body: supports circulation, digestion and healthy skin.
  • Brain: helps concentration, mood and alertness.
  • Kidneys: flush out waste and prevent infection.

How much fluid?

Most adults need around 1.5 to 2 litres of fluid a day — roughly six to eight glasses — and more in hot weather, when active, or when unwell with a fever, vomiting or diarrhoea. Individual needs vary, so always follow the person's care plan.

Almost all drinks count towards this, including water, milk, squash, fruit juice, tea and coffee. Foods with a high water content, such as soup, jelly, yoghurt and fruit, help too. The aim is a steady intake spread across the whole day rather than a lot in one go, so keeping drinks flowing from morning to evening is the best way to help someone stay well hydrated.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap.
items.textField: Most adults should aim for around *six* to *eight* glasses of fluid a day, which is roughly *1.5* to *2* *litres*.

items.html:
Spotting dehydration

Dehydration often creeps up quietly, so it helps to know the signs. Watching urine is one of the simplest checks: pale urine usually means someone is well hydrated, while dark, strong-smelling urine is an early warning that they need more to drink.

  • Early signs: dark urine, dry mouth and lips, thirst, headache.
  • Feeling unwell: tiredness, dizziness, constipation.
  • Serious signs: confusion, sunken eyes, drowsiness, few wet trips to the toilet.

Why dehydration is dangerous

Dehydration is not a minor problem. It can trigger urinary and chest infections, constipation, pressure ulcers, falls and sudden confusion, and it can make existing conditions much worse. Severe dehydration can lead to kidney problems and hospital admission.

In older people the warning is that confusion, drowsiness or an unexplained fall can be the first sign of dehydration, so never dismiss these changes as simply part of getting older. Because dehydration is so much easier to prevent than to treat, the best approach is always to help people drink well before problems appear, and to act quickly at the first signs.

items.question: Which of these is an early sign that someone may be becoming dehydrated?

answers.text: Dark, strong-smelling urine together with a dry mouth and thirst.
answers.feedback: Yes. Dark urine and a dry mouth are common early warnings that someone needs more to drink.

answers.text: Pale, plentiful urine and clear, moist lips.
answers.feedback: These are signs of good hydration, not dehydration.

answers.text: Feeling too full after a large drink.
answers.feedback: This is not a sign of dehydration.

answers.text: Sleeping well and having plenty of energy.
answers.feedback: Dehydration tends to cause tiredness and low energy, not the opposite.

items.html:
Who is most at risk

Some people are far more likely to become dehydrated and need extra support and closer watching. As we age, the sense of thirst becomes weaker, so many older people simply do not feel thirsty even when their body needs fluid.

  • Older people: a reduced sense of thirst.
  • People with dementia or confusion: may forget to drink or forget they are thirsty.
  • People who need help: those who cannot reach or hold a drink unaided.

Barriers to drinking

It is worth understanding why someone might not drink enough, because the reason often points to the solution. Some people deliberately drink less because they worry about needing the toilet often or about incontinence — reassuring them and supporting toileting can help.

Others struggle because a drink is out of reach, because swallowing is difficult, or because they cannot easily ask. People who find communication hard, or who are unwell with a fever, sickness or diarrhoea, or who are nearing the end of life, all need extra attention to their fluids. Knowing who on your shift is most at risk helps you make sure their drinks are never forgotten.

items.question: Some people deliberately limit how much they drink. What is a common reason for this?

answers.text: They worry about needing the toilet often, or about incontinence.
answers.feedback: Yes. Reassurance and good toileting support often help people feel able to drink more.

answers.text: They have been told that fluids are bad for their health.
answers.feedback: Fluids are essential; this is not a usual reason.

answers.text: They no longer need any fluid once they reach old age.
answers.feedback: Everyone needs fluids throughout life — older people are actually more at risk.

answers.text: Drinking less has no effect on the body.
answers.feedback: Drinking too little quickly leads to dehydration and its many risks.

items.html:
Making drinking easier

Small, thoughtful changes make a real difference to how much someone drinks. Offer drinks regularly throughout the day rather than only at mealtimes, and offer drinks people actually like, at a temperature they enjoy.

  • Offer often: little and often, and always leave a drink within reach.
  • Right drink: a favourite drink at the right temperature.
  • Right cup: a cup, beaker or aid the person can manage.

Fluid charts

When someone is at risk of dehydration, the team may keep a fluid balance chart that records what the person drinks across the day, and sometimes their output too. This helps everyone see at a glance whether the person is getting enough.

If you are asked to complete one, record each drink accurately and at the time, note the amount actually taken rather than the amount offered, and tell a senior colleague if the person is not reaching their target. An accurate chart is only useful if everyone fills it in honestly, so never guess or write it up later from memory. Foods such as jelly, ice lollies, custard and soup can also help top up fluids for someone who is reluctant to drink.

A balanced diet

A balanced diet gives the body the range of nutrients it needs to stay healthy. The NHS Eatwell Guide shows the proportions of different food groups that make up a healthy diet for most adults. The key ideas are variety, balance and enjoyment.

  • Variety: foods from each of the main groups.
  • Balance: the right proportions, not too much of any one thing.
  • Enjoyment: meals the person likes and looks forward to.

The Eatwell Guide

The Eatwell Guide suggests plenty of fruit and vegetables, meals based on starchy foods such as potatoes, bread, rice and pasta (wholegrain where possible), some protein like beans, pulses, fish, eggs and meat, and some dairy or dairy alternatives. It also advises small amounts of unsaturated oils, and less sugar, salt and saturated fat.

Remember, though, that standard healthy-eating advice is not right for everyone. People who are underweight or at risk of malnutrition often need higher-calorie, nourishing food rather than low-fat or low-sugar choices, so always follow the individual's care plan. The Eatwell Guide is a helpful picture of a balanced diet for the general population, but the person in front of you and their assessed needs always come first.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap.
items.textField: The Eatwell Guide suggests we eat at least *five* portions of fruit and vegetables a day, base meals on *starchy* foods, choose *wholegrain* options where possible, and include beans, pulses and *fish* as protein.

items.html:
Understanding malnutrition

Malnutrition means the body is not getting enough energy and nutrients to stay healthy. It is common in older and unwell people, and it often develops slowly and unnoticed. Spotting it early gives the best chance of putting things right.

  • What it is: too little energy, protein or nutrients over time.
  • Causes: poor appetite, illness, low mood, dementia or swallowing problems.
  • Signs: weight loss, loose clothes or dentures, tiredness, poor healing.

Screening and monitoring

To pick up malnutrition, many services use a screening tool called MUST (the Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool, from BAPEN). It helps identify adults who are malnourished or at risk, so they can get the right support.

You may be asked to weigh people regularly, watch for unplanned weight loss and record what someone eats on a food chart. These records help the team see whether someone is eating enough over time, and they support decisions about fortified food or supplements. Report any concerns — such as clothes becoming loose, dentures no longer fitting or meals left unfinished — to a nurse or dietitian so they can act. Left unnoticed, malnutrition slows recovery, weakens the body and raises the risk of falls and infection.

items.question: What is the MUST tool used for?

answers.text: Screening adults to identify those who are malnourished or at risk of malnutrition.
answers.feedback: Correct. MUST flags who needs further assessment and support around nutrition.

answers.text: Measuring a person's blood pressure.
answers.feedback: MUST is about nutrition risk, not blood pressure.

answers.text: Recording how much fluid a person drinks.
answers.feedback: That is a fluid balance chart. MUST screens for malnutrition risk.

answers.text: A safe method for moving and handling people.
answers.feedback: MUST is a malnutrition screening tool, not a moving and handling technique.

items.html:
Different needs, different diets

People need different diets for many reasons, and it is your job to follow what the care plan sets out rather than to decide for yourself. Some diets are prescribed for a health condition, others reflect a person's beliefs, and some exist to keep a person safe.

  • Therapeutic diets: such as diabetic, low-salt, renal or gluten-free for coeliac disease.
  • Cultural and religious: such as halal, kosher, vegetarian, vegan or fasting.
  • Allergies: foods that must be avoided completely to stay safe.

Fortifying food and respecting choice

For people at risk of malnutrition, the plan may involve fortifying food — adding extra calories and protein using everyday foods like whole milk, cream, cheese or milk powder — along with nourishing snacks, offering food little and often, and any supplements the dietitian has prescribed.

Respecting cultural and religious food needs is a central part of person-centred care, not an optional extra, and getting it right shows real respect for who a person is. Allergies and intolerances must be taken very seriously indeed: a food allergy can be life-threatening, so always follow the person's recorded allergy information, check ingredients when you are unsure, and never assume or guess. If you are ever in doubt about what someone can safely eat, stop and check with a senior colleague first.

Making mealtimes better

How a meal is served matters as much as what is on the plate. Person-centred mealtime support starts with offering genuine choice, creating a pleasant, calm environment, and giving people the time and help they need without taking over.

  • Choice: what to eat, how much, and where to sit.
  • Environment: calm, unhurried and free of clutter and distractions.
  • Positioning: sitting upright and comfortable to eat safely.

Support and independence

Many services use protected mealtimes, where non-urgent tasks pause so staff can focus on helping people eat. When you assist someone, sit down at their eye level, go at their pace and never rush them.

Encourage people to do as much as they can for themselves, and use assistive equipment where it helps — adapted cutlery, plate guards, non-slip mats and special cups can all support independence and dignity. Little touches matter too, such as describing the food to someone with poor sight, offering one course at a time, and making sure glasses, dentures and hearing aids are in place. Good positioning, sitting the person upright rather than slumped or lying back, also helps them swallow safely.

items.question: When helping someone to eat, you should sit down at their level, go at their pace and encourage them to do as much as they can for themselves.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Sitting at their level, not rushing, and supporting independence keeps mealtimes safe and dignified.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Sitting down, going at the person's pace and supporting their independence is good person-centred practice.

items.html:
Swallowing difficulties

Dysphagia is the word for difficulty swallowing. It is common after a stroke, and in dementia and some other conditions. It is serious because it can lead to choking, and to food or drink going into the lungs (aspiration), which can cause chest infections and pneumonia.

  • What it is: difficulty or discomfort when swallowing.
  • Warning signs: coughing or choking when eating or drinking, a wet or gurgly voice.
  • Other clues: food held in the mouth, recurrent chest infections, weight loss.

SALT and IDDSI

If you notice signs of swallowing difficulty, report them so the person can be referred to a speech and language therapist (SALT), who assesses swallowing and advises on how to keep the person safe.

A SALT may recommend texture-modified food and thickened drinks, described using the IDDSI framework, which sets out standard, numbered levels for how thick a drink or how soft a food should be. This gives everyone a shared language so the same texture is served every time. Always give the exact texture and thickness written in the care plan — giving the wrong one, such as a thin drink to someone who needs thickened fluids, or a normal meal to someone who needs it pureed, can cause choking and is genuinely dangerous. If you are unsure of someone's level, check before you serve.

items.question: A person you support keeps coughing and sounds wet and gurgly when they drink. What should you do?

answers.text: Report it, as these can be signs of a swallowing difficulty (dysphagia) that needs a SALT assessment.
answers.feedback: Yes. Coughing and a wet, gurgly voice are warning signs that should always be reported.

answers.text: Decide yourself to thicken all their drinks from now on.
answers.feedback: Never change textures yourself — a SALT must assess and set the right level first.

answers.text: Encourage them to drink faster to get it over with.
answers.feedback: Rushing increases the risk of choking and aspiration.

answers.text: Ignore it, as coughing during drinks is completely normal.
answers.feedback: Regular coughing when drinking is not normal and should be reported.

items.html:
Safety, recording and speaking up

Keeping people safe around food and drink brings together good hygiene, careful recording and the confidence to raise concerns. Safe food handling is one basic principle — wash your hands, store food correctly, check use-by dates and never serve food that may be unsafe.

  • Safe food handling: clean hands, correct storage, check dates — covered fully in the Food Hygiene course.
  • Accurate records: complete food and fluid charts honestly and at the time.
  • Report concerns: raise changes early with a senior colleague or nurse.

Bringing it together

Throughout your work, keep monitoring how people eat and drink, watch for weight loss and record what you see on food and fluid charts. If someone is refusing food or drink, losing weight, or coughing when they swallow, report it promptly so the right professional can help — small changes noticed early can prevent serious harm.

Remember: this course covered the knowledge element of Standard 8 only. Your employer must also observe your competence at work and sign it off — My Care Academy cannot award the Care Certificate on its own.

items.question: You notice that someone has eaten almost nothing for two days and has started coughing when they drink. What is the right action?

answers.text: Report your concerns to a senior colleague or nurse straight away, and record what you have observed.
answers.feedback: Correct. Prompt reporting and clear recording get the person the assessment and help they need.

answers.text: Wait a few more days to see whether things improve on their own.
answers.feedback: Waiting risks serious harm. Both poor intake and coughing when drinking need reporting now.

answers.text: Change their meals and drinks yourself without telling anyone.
answers.feedback: Changes to diet or fluid texture must be led by the right professional, not decided alone.

answers.text: Assume they are just not hungry and move on.
answers.feedback: Two days of eating almost nothing plus coughing are clear warning signs to escalate.
